messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_ashrae2019.pdf
Equipment class: cooling_tower

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_043f74c93138d20b", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Warehouse Ventilation Rate", "summary": "Standard 62.1 recommends 0.3 L/(s·m²) of ventilation as a design criterion for warehouse ventilation.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Minimum outdoor air ventilation rate for general warehouses", "title": "Warehouse Ventilation Rate", "summary": "Standard 62.1 recommends 0.3 L/(s·m²) of ventilation as a design criterion for warehouse ventilation."}, "evidence": "Licensed for single user. © 2019 ASHRAE, Inc.3.8 2019 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications (SI)
Cruise Terminals. Ventilation design must ensure that fumes
and odors from forklifts and cargo in work areas do not penetrate
occupied and administrative areas.
Bus Terminals. The primary concerns with enclosed bus loading
areas are health and safety probl ems, which must be handled by
proper ventilation (see Chapter 16). Although diesel  engine fumes
are generally not as noxious as ga soline fumes, bus terminals often
have many buses loading and unloading at the same time, and thetotal amount of fumes and odors may be disturbing.
In terms of health and safety, enclosed bus loading areas and
automobile parking garages pres ent the most serious problems.
Three major problems are encountered , the first and most serious of
which is emission of carbon monoxide (CO) by cars and oxides of
nitrogen (NO
x) by buses, which can caus e serious illness and pos-
sibly death.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_ed0d6e62569d5c17", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Warehouse Humidity Control in Humid Climates", "summary": "In humid climates, infiltration of moist air can cause unacceptably high space humidity when cooling equipment is off due to low sensible load.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Humidity control challenge", "title": "Warehouse Humidity Control in Humid Climates", "summary": "In humid climates, infiltration of moist air can cause unacceptably high space humidity when cooling equipment is off due to low sensible load."}, "evidence": "WAREHOUSES AND DISTRIBUTION CENTERS
General Design Considerations
Warehouses can be defined as facilities that provide proper envi-
ronment for the purpose of storing goods and materials. They are
also used to store equipment and material inventory at industrialfacilities. At times, warehouses  may be open to the public. The
buildings are generally not air conditioned, but often have sufficient
heat and ventilation to provide a tolerable working environment. In
many cases, associated facilities occupied by office workers, such as
shipping, receiving, and inventor y control offices, are air condi-
tioned. Warehouses must be designed to accommodate the loads of
materials to be stored, associated  handling equipmen t, receiving and
shipping operations and associated trucking, and needs of operating
personnel.", "source_pages": [1, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_29cc76c6f97bb550", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Fume Hood Type for Perchloric Acid", "summary": "Perchloric acid hoods require special construction with internal washdown systems and materials resistant to acid attack due to explosion hazard.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Perchloric acid hood requirements", "title": "Fume Hood Type for Perchloric Acid", "summary": "Perchloric acid hoods require special construction with internal washdown systems and materials resistant to acid attack due to explosion hazard."}, "evidence": "Licensed for single user. © 2019 ASHRAE, Inc.3.8 2019 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications (SI)

and odors from forklifts and cargo in work areas do not penetrate
occupied and administrative areas.

areas are health and safety probl ems, which must be handled by
proper ventilation (see Chapter 16). Although diesel  engine fumes
are generally not as noxious as ga soline fumes, bus terminals often
have many buses loading and unloading at the same time, and thetotal amount of fumes and odors may be disturbing.
In terms of health and safety, enclosed bus loading areas and
automobile parking garages pres ent the most serious problems.

which is emission of carbon monoxide (CO) by cars and oxides of
nitrogen (NO

In terms of health and safety, enclosed bus loading areas and

Three major problems are encountered , the first and most serious of

x) by buses, which can caus e serious illness and pos-
sibly death.", "source_pages": [1, 3], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_e6c6d9532b0e839c", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Vapor Pressure Differential and Diffusion", "summary": "Vapor diffusion occurs from higher to lower vapor pressure and can cause structural damage depending on climate and interior conditions.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Vapor diffusion mechanism", "title": "Vapor Pressure Differential and Diffusion", "summary": "Vapor diffusion occurs from higher to lower vapor pressure and can cause structural damage depending on climate and interior conditions."}, "evidence": "Air leakage or gaps speed heat transfer, but may
be defeated for thermal loads with positive pressurization. Vapor
carried by air cannot be fully miti gated pressurizati on: it slows the
process by slowing air transfer, but vapor flow and equilibration
between interior and exterior spac es continue inde pendently based
on vapor, rather than air, pressu re. The differential between interior
and exterior vapor pressures also  drives diffusion (movement of
water vapor through permeable ma terials) from areas of higher
vapor pressure to lower. Diffus ion manifests diffe rently depending
on exterior climates and interior environments. When exterior mois -
ture conditions are higher than inte rior con ditions, wh ether typically
or seasonally, vapor may move inward, especially during dehumid-
ification, adding to system load and causing efflorescen ce or other
damage on interior surfaces.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_2ff95e7e7dcd3a9e", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Condensation Risks on Windows/Doors", "summary": "Windows, doors, and skylights can pose risks as potential condensation points and points of heat transfer, leading to mold, rot, or masonry damage.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Condensation risk elements", "title": "Condensation Risks on Windows/Doors", "summary": "Windows, doors, and skylights can pose risks as potential condensation points and points of heat transfer, leading to mold, rot, or masonry damage."}, "evidence": "Appr opriate zoning should minimize the
necessity for downstream subzone equipment (VAV/reheat, subzone
humidification) in most occupied  and unoccupied collections zones.
Activities that pose a potential threat to collections environments
(food areas, loading docks, fabr ication and rough shops, mainte -
nance and housekeeping, etc.) should be kept away from collec -
tions zones, with exhaust from  these areas located away and
downwind from any fresh air inta kes for collections systems and
the rest of the building. One key exception to this practice may be
relative-humidity-controlled crate storage, which may share a zone
with occupied collections areas.  Loading docks should ideally be
positively pressurized, and shared  walls with collections zones
should be carefully designed to mitigate any potential energy trans -
fer, whether from a load perspective or to red uce risk of condensation
or other issues.", "source_pages": [3, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_d524cac12b2a9921", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Latent Capacity in Cultural Heritage Systems", "summary": "Adequate latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs, causing relative humidity to rise beyond safe limits as exterior temperatures climb.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Latent capacity requirement", "title": "Latent Capacity in Cultural Heritage Systems", "summary": "Adequate latent capacity is often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs, causing relative humidity to rise beyond safe limits as exterior temperatures climb."}, "evidence": "Oil and gasoline fu mes, which may cause nausea and
headaches and can create a fire haza rd, are also of concern. The third
issue is lack of air movement and the resulting stale atmosphere
caused by increased CO content in  the air. This condition may cause
headaches or grogginess. Most codes require a min imum ventilation
rate to ensure that the CO concen tration does not exceed safe limits.
Chapter 16 covers ventilation requirements and calculation proce-
dures for enclosed vehicula r facilities in detail.
All underground garages should have facilities for testing the CO
concentration or should have the garage checked periodically. Prob-
lems such as clogged duct syst ems; improperly operating fans,
motors, or dampers; or clogged ai r intake or exha ust louvers may
not allow proper air circulation.  Proper maintenance is required to
minimize any operational defects.

sibly death.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_f88d5adc9dac6bc4", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Air Curtain Effectiveness Range", "summary": "Research shows air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%, depending on type and application.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Air curtain effectiveness", "title": "Air Curtain Effectiveness Range", "summary": "Research shows air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%, depending on type and application."}, "evidence": "It is represented as a percent-
age, and the amount of energy sa ved is reduced by the energy con-
sumed by the unit. Research (e.g ., Pappas and Tassou [2003]) shows
that air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%,
depending on the type and application.
To fully realize benefi ts of using an air curtain unit, make sure
that equipment is prope rly sized, installed, adjusted, and main-
tained.
Application Considerations
Air curtain unit selection depe nds on the opening’s width and
height. To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must at leastcover or slightly overlap the entire opening, and have a minimum
velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface (Wang and Zhong
2014). The unit is usually mounted above or beside a door or windowopening. When mounted above the opening, the horizontal air cur-
tain unit discharges its air verti cally down across the opening.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_bdea4ab804182880", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Air Curtain Minimum Velocity", "summary": "To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must cover the opening and have a minimum velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Air curtain velocity requirement", "title": "Air Curtain Minimum Velocity", "summary": "To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must cover the opening and have a minimum velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface."}, "evidence": "It is represented as a percent-
age, and the amount of energy sa ved is reduced by the energy con-
sumed by the unit. Research (e.g ., Pappas and Tassou [2003]) shows
that air curtains have a range of effectiveness from 60 to 90%,
depending on the type and application.
To fully realize benefi ts of using an air curtain unit, make sure

tained.

Air curtain unit selection depe nds on the opening’s width and
height. To maximize effectiveness, the air curtain unit must at leastcover or slightly overlap the entire opening, and have a minimum
velocity projection of 2 m/s at the target surface (Wang and Zhong
2014). The unit is usually mounted above or beside a door or windowopening. When mounted above the opening, the horizontal air cur-
tain unit discharges its air verti cally down across the opening.", "source_pages": [6], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_e79e544e16bacc6d", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Non-Recirculating vs. Recirculating Air Curtains", "summary": "Non-recirculating air curtain units are less expensive and have few installation limitations, while recirculating systems have higher effectiveness but are more expensive and require careful integration.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Air curtain construction types", "title": "Non-Recirculating vs. Recirculating Air Curtains", "summary": "Non-recirculating air curtain units are less expensive and have few installation limitations, while recirculating systems have higher effectiveness but are more expensive and require careful integration."}, "evidence": "Oil and gasoline fu mes, which may cause nausea and

All underground garages should have facilities for testing the CO

lems such as clogged duct syst ems; improperly operating fans,
